source.title: Bidirectional switch of the valence associated with a hippocampal contextual memory engram

confidence_rationale: Direct optogenetic manipulation of engram cells in DG and BLA with valence reversal paradigm. High source quality (Nature, Tonegawa lab, rigorous controls). The key finding — DG engrams are valence-flexible while BLA engrams are valence-fixed — maps directly onto the REE distinction between hippocampal terrain (which should be updatable) and amygdala valence tagging (which is more rigid). Moderate mapping fidelity because REE's wanting/liking distinction is not identical to the fear/reward reversal tested here, but the structural principle (hippocampus = flexible context, amygdala = fixed valence) is directly relevant.
failure_signatures: BLA engrams could not be switched from fear to reward or vice versa — this challenges any architecture that assumes amygdala valence representations are as malleable as hippocampal ones

mapping.source_claim_statement: Hippocampal dentate gyrus engram cells exhibit plasticity such that the valence of a conditioned response evoked by their reactivation can be reversed by re-associating the contextual memory with an opposite-valence unconditioned stimulus. Basolateral amygdala engram cells do not exhibit this plasticity.
mapping.ree_translation: The hippocampal terrain in REE (ARC-007) must support valence updating — a location previously associated with harm can become associated with wanting, and vice versa. This is consistent with hippocampal engrams being valence-flexible. The amygdala's role is to provide fixed valence tags (liking/dread) that the hippocampus can associate with context but that are themselves less plastic. MECH-229's wanting/liking dissociation maps onto the different plasticity properties: wanting (hippocampal, flexible, updatable) vs liking (more amygdala-adjacent, consummatory, fixed at the moment of contact).
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper uses optogenetic reactivation of labelled engram populations, not natural navigation or reward-seeking behaviour. The valence 'switch' is achieved by forced co-activation of an engram with a new US, not by the organism's own experience. REE's wanting/liking distinction does not perfectly align with the fear/reward paradigm used here.
mapping.source_context: Mice, optogenetic ChR2 labelling of DG or BLA engram cells during fear or reward conditioning, followed by cross-valence reconditioning.

confidence_components.notes: Very high source quality. Mapping fidelity moderate because the paper tests engram valence switching rather than navigation or wanting/liking per se. Transfer risk low — the structural principle (DG flexible, BLA fixed) is likely conserved.
